question: State and explain the Church-Turing thesis.
expectedPoints: The thesis asserts that any function that is effectively computable can be computed by a Turing machine.
It identifies the intuitive notion of an 'algorithm' with the formal model of a Turing machine.
It serves as a fundamental postulate, not a theorem, that defines the limits of mechanical computation.

question: Explain the significance of the Halting Problem in the Theory of Computation.
expectedPoints: The Halting Problem is the problem of deciding whether a given Turing machine will halt on a given input.
Alan Turing proved it is undecidable, meaning no general algorithm exists to solve it for all possible machine-input pairs.
This was one of the first and most important examples of an undecidable problem, establishing fundamental limits on what can be computed.

question: Describe the classes P and NP. Explain the P vs. NP problem, its significance, and why it remains one of the most important open problems in computer science.
expectedPoints: P is the class of decision problems solvable by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time.
NP is the class of decision problems for which a proposed solution can be verified by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time.
The P vs. NP problem asks whether every problem in NP is also in P (i.e., P = NP).

Its significance lies in its implications: if P = NP, many problems currently considered intractable could be solved efficiently, revolutionizing fields like cryptography, optimization, and artificial intelligence.
It remains unsolved because no one has been able to prove that P ≠ NP or find a polynomial-time algorithm for an NP-complete problem.
